title: The Falsified Medicines Directive Explained
author: B. Subramanian
excerpt: A clear guide to the Falsified Medicines Directive: safety features, the unique identifier, the GB versus NI split, and what UK quality teams must do.
cover_image_prompt: A photorealistic clinical-luxury pharmaceutical banner in a cool teal and cyan palette over deep navy, lit by soft natural light. A gloved hand inspects a sealed medicine carton bearing an unmarked 2D data-matrix square and an intact tamper-evident seal, held above a polished laboratory bench; a blurred handheld scanner glows faintly in the background, suggesting authentication at the point of supply. Shallow depth of field isolates the carton and seal against cool reflective surfaces, with a single subtle green accent catching the edge of the scanner. Fine condensation and clean shadows convey precision, security and trust. Composition is 4:3, elegant and uncluttered, with no text, no logos and no readable characters anywhere in the frame.

faqs.q: Does the Falsified Medicines Directive still apply in the UK after Brexit?
faqs.a: It is split. The safety-features and decommissioning requirements no longer apply in Great Britain, where wholesalers and pharmacies are not required to scan or decommission packs against the EU repositories. They do, however, continue to apply in Northern Ireland, which remains aligned with the relevant EU rules. A single business may therefore need two distinct procedures depending on whether goods are destined for GB or NI.

faqs.q: What are the two safety features required under the FMD?
faqs.a: The first is an anti-tampering device on the outer packaging that lets anyone in the chain see whether the pack has been opened or altered. The second is a unique identifier, a serial number and product data encoded in a 2D data matrix and held in national and European repositories. Together they allow a pack's authenticity to be verified and its identifier decommissioned at the point of dispensing.

faqs.q: What is decommissioning under the Falsified Medicines Directive?
faqs.a: Decommissioning is the act of checking a pack's unique identifier against the repository and then retiring that identifier so it cannot be reused, normally carried out at the point of dispensing. It confirms the pack is genuine and has not already been supplied elsewhere. In Great Britain this step is no longer required, whereas in Northern Ireland it continues to apply under the EU-aligned regime.
